diary: Woke up to that steady, drizzly gray—Renton never really does “spring sunshine” when it’s like this. I did my little mobility routine, made coffee, and checked the ferry and weather like I always do, partly habit and partly because the rain makes my brain want a plan. Breakfast was quick at Father’s Office Cafe on Capitol Hill, then I headed toward Kirkland with extra buffer time. Around Totem Lake, the chatter about the SR-520 eastbound closure was already in the air, so I took it slow and kept my shoulders relaxed. At the Kirkland Sports Physical Therapy Clinic, we stayed indoors and I worked with patients on gait and balance—lots of structured reps, lots of small wins. Lunch at Whole Foods felt warm and sheltered. After work, I grabbed board-game snacks and tea supplies from QFC, then came home, showered, and set up chess puzzles. Tonight’s laughter with friends made the whole day feel lighter, even under the rain.
thought_process: I chose the least annoying route because the SR-520 eastbound closure (9am–noon) would ripple into everything—arrivals, appointments, even how patient families planned their day. That’s why I built in buffer time and re-checked road status after lunch, even though it’s redundant; it keeps me from getting tense. Once I got to the clinic, I leaned into structure—gait and balance work is easier when the session is consistent, especially on a rainy day when everyone’s energy feels a little damp. The drizzly weather definitely softened my mood: I felt quieter on the drive back, but I also made room for cozy routines at home—tea, snacks, chess puzzles. Hosting friends later shifted me back into myself, calmer and more hopeful.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
